title: The transformation of Finland's Baltic Sea strategy
gist: Finland's Baltic Sea strategy has moved through Cold War neutrality, post-Soviet regional cooperation with Russia, and now full NATO integration after 2023; with the Baltic effectively a 'NATO lake', Finland faces a new regime of hybrid threats to undersea infrastructure while still anchoring policy in stability and international law.

insights.claim: Over 80% of Finland's foreign trade is seaborne through the Baltic, and after the 2022 Russo-Ukrainian war and border closure that share has risen to 95%.
insights.speaker: Tuomas Forsberg
insights.so_what: Quantifies Finland's near-total economic exposure to Baltic sea-lane security, a structural vulnerability.
insights.quote: the proportion has risen to 95 per cent

insights.claim: Finland's Baltic policy passed through three phases: Cold War neutrality balancing East and West, post-Soviet regional cooperation including with Russia, and post-2022 full integration into Western security structures.
insights.speaker: Tuomas Forsberg
insights.so_what: A clean periodisation for tracking how a frontline state adapts to shifting great-power balance.
insights.quote: from cautious neutrality during the Cold War, to active regional cooperation after the fall of the Soviet Union, to full integration into the western security structures
insights.locator: Finland's Baltic Sea policy has undergone a clear transformation

insights.claim: The aim of Cold War 'Finlandisation' was never genuine friendship but the protection of core state sovereignty and national interests, with friendship often just lip service.
insights.speaker: Tuomas Forsberg
insights.so_what: Reframes accommodation policy as instrumental sovereignty-protection, not subordination.
insights.quote: The real aim of the state leadership was not friendship as such

insights.claim: Finland and Sweden's NATO entry has made the Baltic effectively a 'NATO lake', though Russia retains use of international waters, giving Finland a new role in regional deterrence.
insights.speaker: Tuomas Forsberg
insights.so_what: Marks a strategic inversion of the Baltic from contested frontier to a Western security zone.
insights.quote: the Baltic has effectively become a 'NATO lake'
insights.locator: Following Finland and Sweden's entry into NATO

insights.claim: Finland applied for NATO membership within months of Russia's February 2022 invasion, having previously kept membership only as a future option, marking an epoch-changing reversal of military non-alignment.
insights.speaker: Tuomas Forsberg
insights.so_what: Shows how fast a deeply held neutrality posture can collapse under an acute security shock.
insights.quote: Finland made a quick decision and applied for membership in NATO in May 2022

insights.claim: Finland is developing a hybrid Nordic-Baltic identity, increasingly converging with the Baltic states' view of Russian society as inherently inclined to use force, while seeking the Norfolk command structure with Nordic countries.
insights.speaker: Tuomas Forsberg
insights.so_what: Signals a hardening, eastern-aligned Finnish threat perception of Russia.
insights.quote: Finland may develop a hybrid Nordic-Baltic identity

insights.claim: Since 2022 incidents in the Baltic have risen sharply, including the Balticconnector gas pipeline and multiple Finland-Estonia, Finland-Germany, Sweden-Lithuania and Sweden-Latvia undersea cables damaged by vessels dragging anchors.
insights.speaker: Tuomas Forsberg
insights.so_what: Concrete evidence of undersea infrastructure as the new contested terrain for hybrid warfare.
insights.quote: were damaged by foreign vessels dragging their anchors on the seabed

insights.claim: These incidents prompted NATO to establish 'Baltic Sentry', an enhanced vigilance activity to protect critical undersea infrastructure and respond if required.
insights.speaker: Tuomas Forsberg
insights.so_what: Names the formal NATO response mechanism to subsea infrastructure threats.
insights.quote: an enhanced vigilance activity called Baltic Sentry

insights.claim: Finland was an active supporter of Nord Stream from the outset because energy cooperation was central to its Northern Dimension initiative, and treated Nord Stream II as an environmental rather than political question.
insights.speaker: Tuomas Forsberg
insights.so_what: Documents how a now-discredited Russia-Germany energy link was depoliticised by participants before 2022.
insights.quote: Nord Stream II was treated not as a political but rather an environmental question in Finland's internal and external policy-making
insights.locator: Finland was, however, an active supporter

insights.claim: Across all eras Finland's constant interest has been promoting regional stability, with reassurance and respect for international law remaining central even amid the deterrence-and-defence shift.
insights.speaker: Tuomas Forsberg
insights.so_what: Identifies the through-line constraining Finnish behaviour even after the strategic pivot.
insights.quote: Finland's key interest has been to promote stability in the region

insights.claim: Finland deliberately avoids offering Russia any casus belli in countering hybrid threats, carefully not breaking international law even as Russia abuses loopholes and challenges norms.
insights.speaker: Tuomas Forsberg
insights.so_what: Shows a deliberate legalist restraint strategy that caps escalation risk.
insights.quote: offering a casus belli is strictly avoided

insights.claim: Finland held that NATO membership would not alter the treaty-based demilitarised status of the Aland Islands, and refrained from expelling the Russian consulate there despite closing others.
insights.speaker: Tuomas Forsberg
insights.so_what: A concrete example of legalist caution constraining even allied hardening.
insights.quote: NATO membership would not affect the current treaty-based status
insights.locator: Another example concerns the precarious status
